idea.title: Prompt-Guided Consistency Regularization for Robust LLM Defenses
idea.problem: Current large language models are vulnerable to adversarial prompt attacks and jailbreak attempts, which can elicit harmful or policy-violating outputs. Existing prompting techniques often fail to generalize against novel or adaptive attack patterns.
idea.motivation: The rapid deployment of LLMs in real-world applications increases the risk of malicious exploitation via adversarial prompting. Enhancing robustness through prompting methods is critical to prevent societal and ethical harms, especially as prompt engineering outpaces manual blacklist or classifier-based interventions.
idea.method: We propose a novel prompting method that injects consistency regularization prompts alongside user inputs. For each user prompt, several automatically generated paraphrases and adversarial variants are constructed using controlled perturbations (e.g., lexical, syntactic, or instruction reversals). The LLM is prompted to respond identically—or explicitly flag inconsistencies—across these variants. At inference, the model's outputs are aggregated using a voting or consistency-checking mechanism, refusing to answer or escalating if inconsistencies or policy violations are detected among responses.
idea.experiment_plan: We will test on adversarial prompt datasets such as AdvBench and JailbreakBench, comparing against standard instruction-following and chain-of-thought prompting as baselines. Metrics will include attack success rate, false positive rate, and response consistency. Human evaluation will assess utility-retaining robustness. Ablations will analyze the impact of different variant generation strategies.

per_idea.text: Prompt-Guided Consistency Regularization for Robust LLM Defenses Current large language models are vulnerable to adversarial prompt attacks and jailbreak attempts, which can elicit harmful or policy-violating outputs. Existing prompting techniques often fail to generalize against novel or adaptive attack patterns. The rapid deployment of LLMs in real-world applications increases the risk of malicious exploitation via adversarial prompting. Enhancing robustness through prompting methods is critical to prevent societal and ethical harms, especially as prompt engineering outpaces manual blacklist or classifier-based interventions. We propose a novel prompting method that injects consistency regularization prompts alongside user inputs. For each user prompt, several automatically generated paraphrases and adversarial variants are constructed using controlled perturbations (e.g., lexical, syntactic, or instruction reversals). The LLM is prompted to respond identically—or explicitly flag inconsistencies—across these variants. At inference, the model's outputs are aggregated using a voting or consistency-checking mechanism, refusing to answer or escalating if inconsistencies or policy violations are detected among responses. We will test on adversarial prompt datasets such as AdvBench and JailbreakBench, comparing against standard instruction-following and chain-of-thought prompting as baselines. Metrics will include attack success rate, false positive rate, and response consistency. Human evaluation will assess utility-retaining robustness. Ablations will analyze the impact of different variant generation strategies.

idea.title: Dynamic Adversarial Prompt Shuffling for Robust Language Model Defense
idea.problem: Standard prompting leaves language models vulnerable to adversarial attacks and jailbreak prompts designed to elicit unsafe or unintended outputs. We lack prompting techniques that proactively disrupt adversarial prompt patterns without degrading normal task accuracy.
idea.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly deployed in public-facing settings, adversarial misuse and jailbreaks have become urgent concerns. Current defenses often require model retraining or rule-based filtering, which are brittle and hard to maintain. Developing prompt-level defenses is a lightweight, model-agnostic way to improve robustness and can coexist with other safety strategies.
idea.method: We propose Dynamic Adversarial Prompt Shuffling (DAPS), which intercepts user prompts and injects stochastic, semantically-preserving transformations (e.g., paraphrasing, order shuffling, synonym substitution) before passing the prompt to the LLM. The method is designed to break adversarial prompt structures that exploit specific token patterns while preserving the underlying intent for benign queries. At inference time, DAPS can adapt transformation intensity based on prompt risk assessment (via a lightweight classifier), maintaining usability for normal users. We train the prompt transformation module adversarially so that it specifically targets known attack vectors.
idea.experiment_plan: We will evaluate DAPS on adversarial prompt datasets (e.g., AdvBench, JailbreakBench) and compare against static filtering and instruction-tuned baselines. Metrics will include attack success rate reduction, benign prompt accuracy/fluency, and user satisfaction (via human evaluation). Ablation studies will assess the impact of each prompt transformation type and adaptive intensity control.

idea.title: Adversarial Shadow Prompting: Self-Reflexive Prompts for Robust LLM Responses
idea.problem: Large language models are highly susceptible to adversarial prompts and jailbreak attacks that elicit unsafe or undesired outputs. Existing prompt engineering and filtering mechanisms can be bypassed by cleverly crafted adversarial inputs.
idea.motivation: As LLMs are deployed in safety-critical and public-facing applications, their robustness to prompt-based attacks becomes paramount. Current defense strategies are reactive, patching known jailbreaks post hoc rather than preventing new attack vectors. A new proactive prompting approach could raise the general robustness frontier.
idea.method: We propose Adversarial Shadow Prompting (ASP), a novel method in which every user prompt is automatically prefixed with a meta-query that instructs the LLM to self-reflect on the intent, ambiguity, and policy compliance of the input. This meta-query is dynamically generated using a secondary prompt that paraphrases the original input in adversarial space—i.e., generates likely exploitative reformulations using an adversarial LLM. The LLM is then required to answer both the shadow prompt (assessing risk and intent) and the user request, and its final output is gated based on the self-reflection. This method creates an internal adversarial check before content generation.
idea.experiment_plan: We will evaluate ASP on existing adversarial and jailbreak benchmark datasets (e.g., AdvBench, JailbreakBench, HarmfulQA). Baselines include standard prompting, prompt filtering, and chain-of-thought self-reflection. Metrics will include attack success rate, false positives, and response helpfulness. Robustness gains will be quantified by the reduction in successful adversarial generations without significant utility loss.

idea.title: Adversarially-Aware Chain-of-Verification Prompting for Robust Large Language Models
idea.problem: Current prompting methods are vulnerable to adversarial attacks and jailbreaking attempts that induce models to output unsafe or policy-violating responses. There is a need for prompting approaches that enhance LLM robustness without retraining.
idea.motivation: As LLMs become widely deployed, adversarial users exploit prompt engineering to circumvent content filters. Prompting methods that anticipate and mitigate these attacks are urgently needed to maintain trust and safety. Prompt-level defenses are attractive because they can adapt quickly and require no model modifications.
idea.method: We propose 'Chain-of-Verification Prompting' (CoVP), where each user request is augmented with a verification stage: the LLM first generates a draft response, then is reprompted (possibly with a different persona or instruction) to explicitly critique or audit the draft for policy violations, adversarial triggers, or unsafe content. The final output is conditioned on both the original and verification stages, producing either a filtered response or a safe reformulation. We design an ensemble of verification personas to increase attack coverage and analyze the effectiveness of iterative self-auditing.
idea.experiment_plan: We will evaluate CoVP on public adversarial prompt datasets (e.g., AdvBench, jailbreak benchmarks) as well as unseen adversarial prompts. Baselines include standard prompting, chain-of-thought, and system instruction approaches. Metrics include attack success rate, harmlessness, and helpfulness (via GPT-4 or human judges). Robustness to unseen attack strategies will also be tested.

idea.title: Counterfactual Calibration Prompts: Leveraging Hypothetical Queries for LLM Uncertainty Estimation
idea.problem: Large language models (LLMs) often produce overconfident outputs, leading to poorly calibrated confidence estimates. Existing prompting methods rarely elicit or quantify model uncertainty explicitly at inference time.
idea.motivation: Accurate confidence calibration is crucial for deploying LLMs in safety-critical and decision-support contexts. As LLMs are increasingly used in applications like medical QA, fact verification, and legal advice, trustworthy uncertainty quantification is more urgent than ever. Current methods primarily rely on output probabilities or post-hoc calibration, lacking direct probing of uncertainty during generation.
idea.method: We propose a novel prompting method that injects explicit counterfactual and adversarial sub-queries alongside the primary query, asking the LLM to simultaneously answer and contrast its confidence between real and subtly perturbed (e.g., negated, swapped, or minimally edited) scenarios. The method operationalizes uncertainty by conditioning the model to reflect on how small changes affect its certainty, producing 'contrastive confidence scores' for each scenario. By aggregating these scores, we estimate the model's calibration and uncertainty per response. This approach can be implemented by template-based prompt engineering and does not require model retraining.
idea.experiment_plan: We will evaluate the method on QA and fact verification benchmarks (e.g., TruthfulQA, FEVER), comparing calibration metrics (ECE, Brier score) and uncertainty prediction quality against standard prompting, softmax-based, and Monte Carlo dropout baselines. Human-annotated sets with known ambiguous or adversarial cases will be used to assess efficacy in challenging scenarios.

idea.title: Uncertainty-Preserving Prompt Ensembles for Calibrated Language Model Confidence
idea.problem: Current prompting techniques often rely on single deterministic prompts or naive prompt ensembles, providing unreliable uncertainty estimates from LLM outputs. This undermines downstream applications requiring calibrated model confidence.
idea.motivation: Accurate uncertainty quantification is crucial for deploying LLMs in risk-sensitive domains such as healthcare, education, or legal advice. Prevailing approaches typically marginalize over discrete prompt templates or soft prompt embeddings, assuming independence and overlooking linguistic ambiguities and context sensitivity. Addressing calibration through more structured prompt perturbations could significantly improve confidence estimation.
idea.method: We propose 'Uncertainty-Preserving Prompt Ensembles' (UPPE): for each input, generate a set of semantically-controlled, ambiguity-injected prompt variants using targeted perturbations grounded in linguistic features (e.g., negation, synonym substitution, syntactic ambiguity). Each variant elicits a model response, and the empirical distribution of responses is used to quantify predictive uncertainty. Unlike naive prompt ensembling, UPPE systematically constructs prompts to expose model epistemic uncertainty, not just variability due to surface form. The final prediction leverages both the consensus and variance across the ensemble to calibrate confidence scores.
idea.experiment_plan: We evaluate UPPE on open-domain QA (e.g., TriviaQA), natural language inference (e.g., ANLI), and medical advice datasets, comparing against standard prompting, self-consistency, and dropout-based uncertainty methods. Metrics include expected calibration error (ECE), Brier score, and coverage on selective prediction. Human evaluation of uncertainty judgments will complement quantitative results.

idea.title: Counterfactual Prompting for Improved Uncertainty Calibration in Large Language Models
idea.problem: Current prompting methods typically quantify model uncertainty via token-level probabilities or ensemble sampling, which often underestimate epistemic uncertainty, especially for out-of-distribution (OOD) or ambiguous queries. This leads to overconfident responses when the model encounters unfamiliar or underrepresented scenarios.
idea.motivation: Effective uncertainty quantification is crucial for deploying LLMs in high-stakes settings, yet existing techniques are poorly calibrated for ambiguous or adversarial prompts. With LLMs increasingly used in open-world contexts, improving confidence estimation is both urgent and underexplored. Prompting strategies that expose model brittleness or epistemic gaps can address this limitation.
idea.method: We propose a novel prompting method: For each input prompt, we automatically generate a set of minimally perturbed counterfactual prompts (e.g., via entity swaps, negation flips, or adversarial insertions) that are semantically close but distinct. We then prompt the model on both the original and its counterfactuals, quantifying answer consistency and entropy across the generated outputs. Model confidence is calibrated based on the variance or disagreement among these outputs, rather than on raw softmax probabilities. The method does not require additional model training or access to internal logits, making it model-agnostic.
idea.experiment_plan: We will evaluate on NLI and QA benchmarks with curated OOD and ambiguous splits (e.g., ANLI, TriviaQA, HANS). Baselines include classical softmax-based uncertainty, temperature scaling, and MC dropout where available. Calibration will be measured using ECE and coverage-error metrics, and downstream utility via selective prediction (abstention) and human alignment assessments.

idea.title: Counterfactual Self-Consistency Prompts for Uncertainty Calibration in Large Language Models
idea.problem: Current prompting methods often rely on single-context inputs and direct model outputs to estimate uncertainty, leading to poorly calibrated confidence scores. There is a lack of mechanisms to directly elicit and quantify internal model uncertainty through prompt engineering.
idea.motivation: Well-calibrated uncertainty is crucial for the safe and reliable deployment of LLMs, especially in high-stakes settings. As models are increasingly used for decision-support, it's essential to robustly quantify when to trust their outputs. Existing calibration methods primarily post-process logits or use ensembling, which are costly or insufficient for deployed prompt-based workflows.
idea.method: We propose 'Counterfactual Self-Consistency Prompting' (CSCP): for any given query, generate a set of counterfactual prompts by perturbing salient input features (e.g., masking keywords, flipping entities, or introducing antonyms). The LLM is prompted to solve both the original and counterfactual queries in a single session, followed by a meta-prompt that asks the model to assess its self-consistency across these variations. This self-assessed consistency score is used as a proxy for epistemic uncertainty, and can be integrated into downstream calibration frameworks.
idea.experiment_plan: We will evaluate CSCP on QA (BoolQ), fact verification (FEVER), and natural language inference (SNLI). Baselines include standard temperature-based and ensemble calibration, as well as chain-of-thought prompting. Metrics include ECE (expected calibration error), Brier score, and selective prediction accuracy. Human evaluation will be used to validate the interpretability of self-consistency scores.

per_idea.raw:
{
  "title": "Counterfactual Self-Consistency Prompts for Uncertainty Calibration in Large Language Models",
  "problem": "Current prompting methods often rely on single-context inputs and direct model outputs to estimate uncertainty, leading to poorly calibrated confidence scores. There is a lack of mechanisms to directly elicit and quantify internal model uncertainty through prompt engineering.",
  "motivation": "Well-calibrated uncertainty is crucial for the safe and reliable deployment of LLMs, especially in high-stakes settings. As models are increasingly used for decision-support, it's essential to robustly quantify when to trust their outputs. Existing calibration methods primarily post-process logits or use ensembling, which are costly or insufficient for deployed prompt-based workflows.",
  "method": "We propose 'Counterfactual Self-Consistency Prompting' (CSCP): for any given query, generate a set of counterfactual prompts by perturbing salient input features (e.g., masking keywords, flipping entities, or introducing antonyms). The LLM is prompted to solve both the original and counterfactual queries in a single session, followed by a meta-prompt that asks the model to assess its self-consistency across these variations. This self-assessed consistency score is used as a proxy for epistemic uncertainty, and can be integrated into downstream calibration frameworks.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate CSCP on QA (BoolQ), fact verification (FEVER), and natural language inference (SNLI). Baselines include standard temperature-based and ensemble calibration, as well as chain-of-thought prompting. Metrics include ECE (expected calibration error), Brier score, and selective prediction accuracy. Human evaluation will be used to validate the interpretability of self-consistency scores."
}

idea.title: Counterfactual Calibration: Prompting LLMs to Self-Evaluate Uncertainty via Synthetic Alternatives
idea.problem: Current prompting methods often conflate model fluency with confidence, leading to unreliable uncertainty estimates. Most methods prompt LLMs for direct answers or self-reported confidence, but these may not reliably reflect actual uncertainty.
idea.motivation: Reliable uncertainty quantification in LLMs is critical for safe deployment in high-stakes settings. Existing approaches are limited by their reliance on uncalibrated likelihoods or subjective self-assessment, motivating new prompting paradigms that surface genuine uncertainty. As LLMs are increasingly used in decision-critical tasks, precise and interpretable uncertainty estimation is more important than ever.
idea.method: We propose Counterfactual Calibration Prompting (CCP): for each input, generate a set of plausible, minimally-perturbed counterfactual prompts (e.g., by editing named entities, quantities, or salient facts). The LLM is prompted to answer both the original and counterfactual queries, then explicitly compare its answers, rating its confidence in distinguishing among them. The divergence and self-comparison across variants are aggregated via structured prompts to yield a granular, context-aware uncertainty estimate, rather than relying on direct probabilities or single-instance self-reports.
idea.experiment_plan: Test CCP on standard QA and fact verification datasets (e.g., TriviaQA, FEVER), comparing uncertainty calibration (ECE, Brier score) and reliability against baselines: direct confidence, MC Dropout, and ensemble methods. Evaluate qualitative and quantitative improvements in uncertainty estimates, especially on ambiguous or adversarial inputs.

idea.title: Self-Contradiction Prompts: Eliciting Uncertainty Estimates via Prompted Consistency Checks
idea.problem: Current LLMs often provide overconfident responses that do not accurately reflect their underlying uncertainty. This undermines their reliability in high-stakes or ambiguous scenarios.
idea.motivation: Accurate uncertainty quantification is essential for deploying LLMs in domains like healthcare or law, yet standard generation does not expose model confidence. Prompt-based approaches have improved LLM capabilities elsewhere, but systematic methods for uncertainty elicitation remain underexplored.
idea.method: We propose 'Self-Contradiction Prompts,' a method that augments standard queries with explicit requests for the model to argue both for and against its own initial answer. Specifically, a prompt first asks the model for its answer, then instructs it to provide the strongest counter-argument or alternative answer, followed by a self-assessment of how certain it is given the opposing viewpoint. This process exposes internal disagreement and allows the model to surface uncertainty as a function of its ability to generate plausible contradictions. The method is designed to be model-agnostic and requires only prompt engineering.
idea.experiment_plan: We will evaluate on QA datasets with known ambiguity (BoolQ, SQuAD Unanswerable, TruthfulQA). Baselines will include vanilla prompting, chain-of-thought, and existing uncertainty-calibration prompts. Metrics will include Brier score, ECE, and correlation between model-expressed and empirical answer accuracy, as well as human assessment of output plausibility.

idea.title: Meta-Prompting: Eliciting Calibrated Uncertainty Quantification from Large Language Models
idea.problem: Large language models (LLMs) often produce overconfident predictions and do not reliably quantify their uncertainty in open-domain question answering. Existing prompting approaches insufficiently calibrate the model's confidence estimates.
idea.motivation: Accurate uncertainty quantification is critical for deploying LLMs in high-stakes domains like healthcare and law, where overconfident errors can have serious consequences. Recent studies show LLMs tend to align their confidence with token likelihoods, which are not always well-calibrated. Improving calibration and uncertainty quantification via prompting offers a scalable solution without retraining models.
idea.method: We propose a meta-prompting approach that first asks the LLM to generate multiple diverse self-consistent answers and corresponding rationales to a given query (e.g., via chain-of-thought or sampling). The prompt then explicitly asks the model to reflect on the consistency and disagreement among its own generated answers, and to provide a structured final output: (1) the most likely answer, (2) a confidence estimate (e.g., probability), and (3) a calibration rationale referring to the observed internal disagreement. This leverages the model's internal diversity to ground confidence in self-assessed uncertainty, rather than raw token probabilities.
idea.experiment_plan: Evaluate on QA datasets with uncertainty labels, such as SQuAD with unanswerable questions or TruthfulQA. Compare to standard single-shot, few-shot, and ensemble prompting baselines. Metrics: calibration error (e.g., ECE), discrimination (AUC), and correlation between model confidence and empirical correctness.

idea.title: Prompt Calibration via Consistency Probes for LLM Uncertainty Quantification
idea.problem: Large language models (LLMs) often produce overconfident answers, even when unsure, and their output probabilities are poorly calibrated. Reliable uncertainty quantification is essential for trustworthy LLM deployment but remains challenging with current prompting techniques.
idea.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used in critical domains (like healthcare and law), miscalibrated confidence can lead to high-stakes errors. Recent work shows LLMs’ confidence scores poorly align with correctness, especially on out-of-distribution or ambiguous inputs. Improved prompt-based calibration could enhance safety and downstream decision-making without retraining models.
idea.method: We propose Consistency Probing Prompts (CPP): for each input, generate several semantically equivalent rephrasings or paraphrases using a controlled prompt. Query the LLM with each version, collect the predicted answers and their probabilities, and use the agreement (consistency) across paraphrases as a prompt-level uncertainty score. We further prompt the model to rate its own confidence per response, combining self-rated and consistency-based signals into a calibrated uncertainty estimate. This method leverages LLMs’ own linguistic flexibility and metacognitive capabilities, requiring no model retraining or architectural changes.
idea.experiment_plan: Evaluate CPP on QA datasets (e.g., TruthfulQA, MMLU, SQuAD) and factuality-sensitive benchmarks. Compare against probability-only and baseline prompting for calibration, using metrics such as Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Brier score, and selective accuracy. Assess performance on both in-domain and distribution-shifted data to test generalization.

idea.title: Counterfactual Self-Reflection Prompts for Calibrating LLM Uncertainty
idea.problem: Large language models often produce overconfident predictions, failing to reliably signal uncertainty or calibrate confidence in their outputs. Current prompting techniques do not leverage the model's own generative process to elicit calibrated uncertainty estimates.
idea.motivation: Robust uncertainty quantification is critical for deploying LLMs in sensitive applications like healthcare or law, where overconfident errors are costly. As LLMs are increasingly used for decision support, enhancing their self-assessment abilities via prompting is timely and necessary.
idea.method: We propose Counterfactual Self-Reflection Prompting (CSRP): for each user query, we prompt the LLM not only for its answer, but also to generate several plausible alternative answers and then reflect on the reasons why these alternatives might be incorrect or less probable than the primary answer. The model is then required to explicitly quantify its confidence in its top answer given the generated alternatives and its own explanations. This leverages the model's internal counterfactual reasoning abilities to calibrate its confidence. All steps are achieved via explicit, structured multi-turn prompts.
idea.experiment_plan: We will evaluate CSRP on multiple QA benchmarks (e.g., TruthfulQA, MMLU, SQuAD) and compare with standard direct-answer and chain-of-thought baselines. Metrics include expected calibration error (ECE), Brier score, and accuracy. We will analyze the correlation between self-reported confidence and correctness, and perform ablation studies to isolate the contribution of counterfactual reasoning.

idea.title: Self-Consensus Prompting: A Prompting Method for Uncertainty Quantification in LLMs
idea.problem: Large language models (LLMs) often produce overconfident responses and do not accurately represent their own uncertainty, making downstream decisions unreliable in high-stakes applications.
idea.motivation: Recent findings show LLMs' token probabilities are poorly calibrated, their confidence is not always correlated with correctness, and repeated generations can yield highly variable answers. Improving uncertainty estimation is crucial for trustworthy AI systems, especially as LLMs are deployed in sensitive domains.
idea.method: We propose Self-Consensus Prompting (SCP): for each query, the prompt instructs the LLM to independently generate multiple reasoning chains (e.g., 'Provide three separate answers, each with your reasoning, and then summarize your agreement and confidence in the final answer.'). The model is prompted to explicitly report its perceived disagreement and justify where uncertainty lies. The aggregate self-assessed agreement and uncertainty, combined with output diversity, serve as an improved uncertainty signal. SCP exploits LLMs' ability for introspection and meta-reasoning, synthesizing both intra-model diversity and explicit self-evaluation.
idea.experiment_plan: We will evaluate SCP on open-domain QA (e.g., TriviaQA), fact verification (e.g., FEVER), and medical QA datasets, comparing against vanilla sampling, majority-voting, and existing uncertainty-estimation techniques. Calibration metrics (e.g., ECE), accuracy-uncertainty correlation, and downstream decision impact will be measured.

idea.title: Eliciting Latent Contradictions: Counterfactual Prompting for Improved Uncertainty Calibration in LLMs
idea.problem: Current prompting and uncertainty quantification methods for LLMs often fail to reveal model uncertainty when outputs are plausible but unsubstantiated. Existing calibration techniques do not directly probe the model’s recognition of alternative plausible answers.
idea.motivation: Better uncertainty quantification is critical for deploying LLMs in high-stakes domains like law and healthcare, where overconfident hallucinations can mislead users. Most existing approaches estimate uncertainty from a single completion or via self-evaluations, missing subtleties in the model's understanding of ambiguous or counterfactual scenarios. Prompting LLMs to engage with alternatives can directly surface epistemic uncertainty, improving reliability.
idea.method: We propose a counterfactual prompting framework: given an input, the LLM first generates its answer and stated confidence. Then, the prompt explicitly asks the LLM to generate plausible alternative answers it finds credible, along with rationales and confidence for each. The distribution and diversity of these alternatives, along with self-assessed confidence, are aggregated into a composite uncertainty score. This approach leverages the LLM’s own latent knowledge about possible contradictory information and ambiguity in the question space.
idea.experiment_plan: We will evaluate our method on open-domain QA (e.g., TriviaQA), medical QA (MedQA), and fact verification (FEVER), comparing against semantic entropy, self-certainty, and best-of-N selection. Metrics include Brier score, Expected Calibration Error, and uncertainty discrimination (AUROC) for detecting unanswerable or ambiguous inputs.

Better uncertainty quantification is critical for deploying LLMs in high-stakes domains like law and healthcare, where overconfident hallucinations can mislead users. Most existing approaches estimate uncertainty from a single completion or via self-evaluations, missing subtleties in the model's understanding of ambiguous or counterfactual scenarios. Prompting LLMs to engage with alternatives can directly surface epistemic uncertainty, improving reliability. We propose a counterfactual prompting framework: given an input, the LLM first generates its answer and stated confidence. The distribution and diversity of these alternatives, along with self-assessed confidence, are aggregated into a composite uncertainty score. This approach leverages the LLM’s own latent knowledge about possible contradictory information and ambiguity in the question space. We will evaluate our method on open-domain QA (e.g., TriviaQA), medical QA (MedQA), and fact verification (FEVER), comparing against semantic entropy, self-certainty, and best-of-N selection. Metrics include Brier score, Expected Calibration Error, and uncertainty discrimination (AUROC) for detecting unanswerable or ambiguous inputs.

idea.title: Contrastive Counterfactual Prompting for Uncertainty Calibration in Large Language Models
idea.problem: Current prompting methods for large language models (LLMs) inadequately quantify model uncertainty, often failing to distinguish between confidently incorrect and genuinely ambiguous responses. There is a need for prompting techniques that elicit more calibrated confidence estimates from LLMs, especially at the granularity of specific alternative answers.
idea.motivation: Reliable uncertainty estimation is crucial for deploying LLMs in high-stakes domains (e.g., medicine, law) where user trust and error mitigation are paramount. Existing methods primarily leverage output probabilities, verbalized confidence, or semantic entropy, but do not probe the model's internal beliefs about plausible alternatives. Developing new prompting strategies that force LLMs to explicitly compare and contrast counterfactuals can improve calibration and robustness.
idea.method: We propose a novel 'Contrastive Counterfactual Prompting' (CCP) method. For a given input, we generate a set of plausible alternative answers (via retrieval or model sampling), then prompt the LLM to (a) evaluate which alternative is most likely correct, (b) provide its relative confidence between the gold answer and each counterfactual, and (c) justify its reasoning. The model's confidence is computed from these relative likelihoods, revealing more granular uncertainty than scalar probabilities or standard self-evaluation. CCP can be automated via prompt templates or chained queries, and directly incorporates structured ambiguity into the prompt.
idea.experiment_plan: We will evaluate CCP on question answering (NaturalQuestions, TriviaQA), fact verification (FEVER), and specialized domains (MedQA) against baselines such as max probability, self-reported confidence, and semantic entropy. Metrics include calibration error (ECE), discrimination (AUC), and hallucination detection. We will analyze both aggregate and per-example improvements, ablation on counterfactual set construction, and robustness to adversarial distractors.

idea.title: Chain-of-Uncertainty: Prompting LLMs for Stepwise Self-Elicitation and Aggregation of Uncertainty in Reasoning
idea.problem: Current LLM uncertainty quantification methods do not explicitly decompose and aggregate token- or step-level uncertainties across multi-step reasoning tasks, limiting their reliability in complex decision-making.
idea.motivation: Multi-step reasoning is increasingly critical for LLM deployment in high-stakes fields, but existing confidence elicitation and calibration approaches focus on single outputs or scalar uncertainty proxies, missing granular, stepwise epistemic signals. More precise, explainable uncertainty propagation can inform users of not only final answer confidence, but also which intermediate steps are fragile. This is important for applications like medical or legal reasoning where traceable uncertainty is crucial.
idea.method: We propose a novel 'Chain-of-Uncertainty' prompting framework: for each reasoning step, the LLM is explicitly prompted to verbalize its confidence (self-rated, e.g., 0-1 scale) and rationale for that estimate, before proceeding to the next step, recursively. Final answer confidence is computed by aggregating these stepwise scores using learned or analytic composition rules. Prompts and aggregation are designed to minimize hindsight bias and enforce local uncertainty estimation. The method leverages LLMs' internal knowledge to provide structured, step-level uncertainty traces, contrasting prior global-only approaches.
idea.experiment_plan: We evaluate on multi-hop QA benchmarks (HotpotQA, StrategyQA), clinical vignettes, and math word problems. Baselines include standard LLM confidence prompts, semantic entropy, rank-calibration, and reward-free best-of-N selection. Metrics include calibration error (ECE), correlation with factual correctness, and qualitative analysis of stepwise uncertainty explanations.

idea.title: Chain-of-Uncertainty Prompting: Eliciting Probability Intervals from LLMs via Stepwise Self-Debate
idea.problem: LLMs often output overconfident or miscalibrated answers, lacking explicit quantification of their own uncertainty beyond softmax or verbalized probability. Existing prompting approaches fail to extract reliable interval-based uncertainty estimates directly from the model's reasoning process.
idea.motivation: Improved uncertainty quantification is critical for trustworthy LLM deployment in safety-critical and high-stake applications. Current approaches relying on entropy, self-certainty, or probability re-scaling do not leverage the model's ability to introspectively generate and debate plausible alternatives before quantifying confidence. Leveraging stepwise self-dialogue could better surface and calibrate model uncertainty.
idea.method: We propose Chain-of-Uncertainty Prompting: a structured prompt template where the LLM first generates an initial answer, then is required (via explicit instruction) to argue for at least two plausible alternative answers, articulating the evidence for or against each. The LLM then assigns a probability interval (e.g., 60–80%) to its initial answer, referencing its own introspective debate. To discourage superficial intervals, the prompt incorporates a consistency check: the LLM must simulate a post-hoc critique of its assigned interval, justifying whether the width is appropriate given the alternatives discussed. This stepwise, self-critical process encourages the model to surface epistemic uncertainty grounded in its own reasoning.
idea.experiment_plan: Evaluate on open-domain QA, medical QA, and arithmetic reasoning datasets. Compare against softmax-based, entropy-based, and best-of-N prompting baselines for calibration error (ECE), interval width, and coverage. Human annotation will assess plausibility of alternatives and appropriateness of intervals. Ablate the self-debate and self-critique stages to isolate their contribution.

idea.title: Prompting Large Language Models with Counterfactual Self-Explanation for Enhanced Uncertainty Calibration
idea.problem: Existing LLM prompts inadequately quantify model uncertainty, leading to overconfident or uncalibrated responses, especially in ambiguous or under-specified contexts. There is a need for prompting methods that elicit more reliable uncertainty estimates directly from LLM outputs.
idea.motivation: Reliable uncertainty quantification is crucial for deploying LLMs in sensitive domains like medicine, law, or education, where overconfident errors pose safety and trust risks. Prior methods focus on entropy proxies, self-consistency, or confidence extraction but do not leverage the model’s ability to self-reflect on alternative plausible answers. Leveraging explicit counterfactual reasoning in prompts may yield more informative and calibrated uncertainty estimates.
idea.method: We propose a two-stage prompting approach: (1) the LLM is prompted to generate not only its best answer, but also to explicitly enumerate one or more plausible counterfactual answers with rationales for each; (2) the LLM is then prompted to self-assess the likelihood or plausibility of each alternative, including its original answer, using a forced-distribution format. This counterfactual self-explanation prompt is designed to activate the model’s internal uncertainty representations by requiring articulation of alternatives and explicit comparative likelihoods, rather than single-point confidence or entropy proxies.
idea.experiment_plan: We will evaluate on tasks from MMLU, TruthfulQA, and MedQA, comparing to prior methods (semantic entropy, self-consistency, rank-calibration, and self-certainty). Calibration will be assessed via ECE, Brier score, and AUROC for error detection. Human evaluation will rate plausibility and informativeness of the generated counterfactuals.

idea.title: Prompt-Stacking Ensembles: Calibrated Uncertainty Estimation via Adversarial Prompt Augmentation
idea.problem: Current large language models (LLMs) are poorly calibrated and tend to be overconfident, especially when faced with adversarial or out-of-distribution (OOD) queries. Prompting methods for uncertainty quantification are rudimentary and do not systematically induce calibrated confidence estimates.
idea.motivation: Reliable uncertainty estimates are critical for deploying LLMs in high-stakes domains like medicine, law, or safety-critical systems. Existing approaches to calibration often require retraining or post-hoc methods and are not robust to prompt variations. There is a pressing need for prompting-based methods that provide well-calibrated confidence scores directly from LLM outputs, especially under adversarial or OOD conditions.
idea.method: We propose a Prompt-Stacking Ensemble (PSE) technique: for each user query, we generate a diverse set of prompts via adversarial augmentation (e.g., paraphrasing, inserting domain shift, or subtle distractors) and pass all variants through the LLM. We aggregate the LLM's logit distributions for each prompt variant and compute entropy-based and consensus-based confidence scores. This ensemble prompt strategy enables direct estimation of epistemic uncertainty, requiring no model modifications. Crucially, we prompt the LLM to self-assess consistency across the prompt ensemble, further refining the confidence calibration.
idea.experiment_plan: We evaluate PSE on QA and fact-checking datasets (e.g., TruthfulQA, MMLU, AdversarialQA), comparing calibration quality (ECE, Brier score) and OOD detection rates against baseline prompting, MC dropout, and temperature-scaling. We ablate prompt set size and adversarial perturbation strength, reporting improvements in calibration error and uncertainty discrimination, especially under adversarial/OOD queries.

idea.title: Self-Aware Prompting: Eliciting Calibrated Uncertainty in Large Language Models via Meta-Confidence Queries
idea.problem: Current prompting methods elicit language model outputs but poorly reflect the model's true confidence or uncertainty, leading to unreliable downstream decisions. There is no prompting technique that directly and accurately quantifies a model's epistemic uncertainty in its predictions.
idea.motivation: Reliable uncertainty quantification is crucial for deploying LLMs in safety-critical applications, but current calibration methods (temperature scaling, MC dropout, etc.) are often post-hoc and dissociated from the actual generation process. Prompting-based calibration could enable plug-and-play, model-agnostic uncertainty estimation, especially important as LLMs are increasingly used in autonomous or high-stakes scenarios. A prompting approach would democratize model calibration, making it accessible to API users without access to model internals.
idea.method: We propose 'Self-Aware Prompting,' a two-stage prompt augmentation where, for a given input, the model is first prompted to generate its best answer as usual. Immediately after, a meta-prompt asks the model to explicitly rate its own uncertainty (e.g., as a probability or with natural language) and to enumerate plausible alternatives with associated confidences. We design meta-prompts that encourage the model to introspect on sources of uncertainty (ambiguity, knowledge gaps) and weight its answers accordingly. This method leverages the model's internal representations via carefully crafted instructions and provides natively calibrated confidence estimates, without external calibration or logit-level access.
idea.experiment_plan: We benchmark Self-Aware Prompting on open-domain QA (e.g., TriviaQA, Natural Questions) and calibrated factuality tasks, comparing against temperature scaling, ensemble, and direct probability estimation baselines. Metrics include ECE/MCE (expected/calibration error), Brier score, and coverage at set confidence thresholds. Human evaluation will assess the plausibility and usefulness of uncertainty explanations.

idea.title: Self-Reflective Uncertainty Prompts: Calibrating LLM Confidence via Chain-of-Confidence Reasoning
idea.problem: Current LLMs frequently produce overconfident or poorly calibrated probabilities for their answers, undermining their reliability in critical domains. There is no robust prompting method that consistently improves calibration without retraining or external models.
idea.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly deployed in safety- and decision-critical settings, well-calibrated confidence estimates are essential. Existing techniques like softmax probabilities or temperature scaling are inadequate for black-box, API-based LLMs. Prompt-based calibration could enable trustworthy uncertainty estimation without architectural changes.
idea.method: We propose Chain-of-Confidence Reasoning (CoCR) prompts, which instruct the LLM to generate an explicit multi-step reasoning trace, then to self-reflect and rate its own confidence in each reasoning step and the final answer. The prompt asks the model to justify each confidence rating in free-text and to estimate a probability for correctness, which is then mapped to the final answer confidence. This method leverages the model's contextual awareness and metacognitive abilities, hypothesizing that explicit self-assessment improves calibration and better reveals true uncertainty.
idea.experiment_plan: We will evaluate CoCR on standard factual QA (e.g., TriviaQA), mathematical reasoning (MATH), and domain-specific medical datasets, comparing against vanilla prompting, vanilla chain-of-thought, and prior uncertainty sampling techniques. Metrics include Brier score, Expected Calibration Error (ECE), and coverage/accuracy trade-offs. Human annotation of confidence justifications will assess fluency and plausibility.

per_idea.text: Self-Reflective Uncertainty Prompts: Calibrating LLM Confidence via Chain-of-Confidence Reasoning Current LLMs frequently produce overconfident or poorly calibrated probabilities for their answers, undermining their reliability in critical domains. There is no robust prompting method that consistently improves calibration without retraining or external models. As LLMs are increasingly deployed in safety- and decision-critical settings, well-calibrated confidence estimates are essential. Existing techniques like softmax probabilities or temperature scaling are inadequate for black-box, API-based LLMs. Prompt-based calibration could enable trustworthy uncertainty estimation without architectural changes. We propose Chain-of-Confidence Reasoning (CoCR) prompts, which instruct the LLM to generate an explicit multi-step reasoning trace, then to self-reflect and rate its own confidence in each reasoning step and the final answer. The prompt asks the model to justify each confidence rating in free-text and to estimate a probability for correctness, which is then mapped to the final answer confidence. This method leverages the model's contextual awareness and metacognitive abilities, hypothesizing that explicit self-assessment improves calibration and better reveals true uncertainty. We will evaluate CoCR on standard factual QA (e.g., TriviaQA), mathematical reasoning (MATH), and domain-specific medical datasets, comparing against vanilla prompting, vanilla chain-of-thought, and prior uncertainty sampling techniques. Metrics include Brier score, Expected Calibration Error (ECE), and coverage/accuracy trade-offs. Human annotation of confidence justifications will assess fluency and plausibility.

idea.title: PromptMix: Mixture-of-Prompts for Uncertainty Calibration in Large Language Models
idea.problem: Current prompting methods for large language models (LLMs) do not yield well-calibrated confidence estimates; softmax probabilities are often overconfident and do not reflect true model uncertainty. This impedes reliable deployment of LLMs in high-stakes or open-ended tasks.
idea.motivation: Accurate uncertainty quantification is critical for LLMs used in contexts like medical QA, legal reasoning, or scientific discovery. Existing approaches often require model retraining, fine-tuning, or post-hoc calibration, which are expensive or infeasible for API-based LLMs. A prompting-based calibration approach would be broadly applicable and immediately actionable.
idea.method: We propose PromptMix, a mixture-of-prompts method where multiple semantically distinct, paraphrased prompts for the same query are issued to the LLM, and its outputs aggregated. The empirical distribution over generated answers is used to estimate uncertainty, and the average self-reported confidence in those outputs is used for calibration. During inference, prompt paraphrases are sampled systematically to ensure diversity, and output variability (e.g., entropy, disagreement) is used as an uncertainty signal. Optionally, a meta-prompt can be added asking the model to self-assess its certainty given the mixture of responses.
idea.experiment_plan: We evaluate PromptMix on multiple QA benchmarks (e.g., TruthfulQA, MedQA) and open-ended reasoning tasks, comparing its uncertainty estimates to vanilla prompting, temperature sampling, and recent post-hoc calibration methods. Metrics include calibration error (ECE), Brier score, and selective risk. As ablations, we compare different paraphrase generation strategies and analyze sensitivity to the number of prompt variants.

idea.title: Self-Consistent Prompting: Eliciting Well-Calibrated Uncertainty in Large Language Models
idea.problem: Current LLMs' confidence estimates, such as softmax probabilities or verbal hedging, are poorly calibrated, making them unreliable for risk-sensitive applications. There is no prompting approach that robustly elicits accurate uncertainty judgements directly from the model.
idea.motivation: Accurate uncertainty quantification is vital for deploying LLMs in medicine, law, and scientific reasoning, where incorrect high-confidence predictions can have serious consequences. As LLMs are increasingly used in decision-making, improved calibration is both urgent and impactful. Traditional post-hoc calibration methods have limited effect due to misalignment between model confidence and true likelihood.
idea.method: We introduce Self-Consistent Prompting (SCP): each question is presented to the LLM alongside a meta-prompt explicitly asking for both an answer and a self-assessment of confidence ('On a scale from 0 to 100, how sure are you of this answer?'). This is repeated across multiple random rephrasings of the input (query perturbations). The model’s uncertainty is then estimated as the variance and average of its self-reported confidences across perturbations, combined with answer consistency. This joint elicitation and aggregation process is designed to regularize over prompt sensitivity and encourage more honest introspection.
idea.experiment_plan: We evaluate on open-domain QA (e.g., TriviaQA, Natural Questions) and fact verification (FEVER) tasks. Baselines include softmax calibration, verbalized hedging, and MC dropout-style perturbations. Calibration is assessed via ECE, Brier score, and proper scoring rules, alongside application-specific safety metrics.

idea.title: Prompting with Calibrated Counterfactuals: Enhancing LLM Uncertainty Quantification via Synthetic Negatives
idea.problem: Large language models tend to be overconfident in their outputs, producing poorly calibrated confidence estimates and unreliable uncertainty quantification, especially in few-shot and zero-shot prompting settings.
idea.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used in risk-sensitive applications, accurate confidence estimation is critical for downstream decisions and user trust. Existing uncertainty quantification methods, such as entropy or prompt ensembles, are limited by the LLM’s inherent biases and lack true calibration, motivating the search for new prompting strategies that make model uncertainty more faithful and actionable.
idea.method: We propose 'Calibrated Counterfactual Prompting', a method that augments standard prompts with strategically generated counterfactual examples—inputs similar to the original prompt but with modified labels or premises designed to elicit model uncertainty. For each input, we create a mixture of original and synthetic hard-negative prompts, and explicitly instruct the LLM to rate its certainty for both positive and counterfactual scenarios. We then aggregate the model's self-reported certainty across the set, using the distributional spread as a calibrated uncertainty signal. This approach leverages the LLM’s contextual awareness to ground its confidence in contrastive, near-miss cases.
idea.experiment_plan: We evaluate on standard NLI and fact verification datasets (e.g., SNLI, FEVER), comparing against strong baselines like ensemble prompting and temperature scaling. Calibration metrics such as Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Brier score, and selective prediction accuracy will be measured. The key experiment is: does our counterfactual prompting technique produce better-calibrated uncertainty estimates (lower ECE, higher coverage at fixed risk) than the best existing prompting-based calibration method?

idea.title: Meta-Prompting: Dynamic Self-Evaluation Prompts for Calibrated Uncertainty in LLMs
idea.problem: Large language models (LLMs) often provide overconfident or poorly calibrated predictions, making it difficult to assess the reliability of their outputs in real time. Existing uncertainty quantification methods rely mainly on ensembling or response sampling, which are computationally expensive and still yield limited calibration.
idea.motivation: Accurate calibration of LLM confidence is critical for high-stakes applications such as medical, legal, or safety-critical decision support. With increased deployment of LLMs in such domains, there is an urgent need for efficient, prompt-based methods to quantify uncertainty without relying on architectural changes or expensive sampling.
idea.method: We propose 'meta-prompting,' a method where LLMs are prompted not only with the original task but also with an explicit self-evaluation request: 'How confident are you in your previous answer, on a scale from 0 to 1?' For every input, the LLM generates both an answer and a calibrated confidence score. During training or alignment, we augment the prompt with selective feedback on calibration, encouraging introspective reasoning. At inference, we aggregate these self-evaluations (optionally across few-shot exemplars) to yield a final uncertainty estimate. This approach leverages the LLM's internal representations for confidence estimation via prompting alone.
idea.experiment_plan: We will evaluate on open-domain QA datasets (e.g., TriviaQA, SQuAD) and factual consistency benchmarks. Baselines include standard response sampling, MC-Dropout (if available), and recent chain-of-thought self-consistency methods. Calibration metrics (ECE, Brier score) and correlation with actual accuracy will be reported. The central experiment: compare the calibration (ECE) and prediction quality of meta-prompting versus baselines, demonstrating superior uncertainty quantification with minimal compute overhead.

idea.title: Prompting for Uncertainty Calibration via Explicit Confidence Queries in LLMs
idea.problem: Large language models (LLMs) often produce overconfident predictions, lacking reliable uncertainty estimates. Existing prompting methods do not effectively elicit calibrated confidence scores for generated answers.
idea.motivation: Accurate confidence calibration is critical for deploying LLMs in high-stakes domains where downstream systems or users must interpret model reliability. As LLMs are increasingly used interactively or for decision support, uncertainty quantification is urgent to prevent unsafe reliance on uncalibrated outputs.
idea.method: We propose 'Explicit Confidence Query Prompting' (ECQP), a two-step prompting method: (1) ask the model for an answer to a question, and (2) immediately follow up in the same context with a standardized meta-prompt that requests the model to quantify its confidence (e.g., 'On a scale from 0 to 1, how confident are you that your previous answer is correct, and why?'). This method leverages the LLM's ability to reason about its own uncertainty and can be further tuned by few-shot demonstrations of calibrated confidence reporting. We compare ECQP to standard single-shot prompting and established uncertainty elicitation prompts.
idea.experiment_plan: We test ECQP on multiple-choice QA datasets with answer keys (e.g., MMLU, TruthfulQA), comparing calibration metrics (ECE, Brier score) against vanilla prompting and the best existing uncertainty elicitation methods (e.g., self-consistency, logit-based confidence). We further evaluate how well the reported confidences separate correct from incorrect answers and analyze explanations for plausibility.

idea.title: Prompting with Counterfactual Queries for Improved LLM Uncertainty Calibration
idea.problem: Large language models (LLMs) often produce overconfident outputs, making it difficult to reliably interpret their uncertainty and confidence scores, especially in open-ended or knowledge-intensive tasks.
idea.motivation: Robust uncertainty estimation is critical for applications in decision-making, safety, and human-AI collaboration. Existing approaches, such as temperature scaling or ensemble methods, offer only limited improvements and are often computationally expensive or require model modifications, underscoring the need for prompt-based solutions that are efficient and model-agnostic.
idea.method: We propose a counterfactual prompting method that, for each input, augments the prompt by systematically querying the LLM with logically incompatible or minimally perturbed counterfactuals (e.g., 'If X were not true, what would you answer?'). The model's distribution over these counterfactual completions is used to derive a calibrated confidence score for the original prediction. This method leverages the LLM's own consistency and ability to reason about alternative possibilities via prompting, without retraining or access to model internals.
idea.experiment_plan: We evaluate on open-domain QA (e.g., TruthfulQA, OpenBookQA) and natural language inference. Baselines include standard logit-based confidence, MC dropout, and self-consistency prompting. Calibration is measured via ECE (Expected Calibration Error) and Brier score. The key experiment is to show that counterfactual prompting yields significantly better-calibrated confidence scores over the strongest self-consistency and temperature-based prompting baselines.

idea.title: Uncertainty-Aware Prompting: Calibrating LLM Confidence via Explicit Abstention Queries
idea.problem: Large language models often output predictions with overconfident probabilities, lacking reliable uncertainty estimates. This impedes safe deployment in settings where knowing when the model is unsure is critical.
idea.motivation: Recent advances in LLMs have achieved impressive accuracy, but their self-reported confidences are poorly calibrated, leading to misinformed decisions. Improving uncertainty quantification is timely as LLMs are increasingly used in high-stakes domains like medicine, law, and code generation.
idea.method: We propose an 'uncertainty-aware prompting' strategy that extends the prompt with explicit instructions allowing the model to abstain or express uncertainty. For each query, the prompt includes: (1) a request for both an answer and a confidence score, (2) an explicit option to reply 'I don't know' or 'uncertain' when appropriate, and (3) a brief context describing when to abstain. This prompt structure is designed to elicit more calibrated uncertainty estimates from the LLM without model finetuning. The predicted confidence is then mapped to a probability and tested for calibration.
idea.experiment_plan: We evaluate on the TruthfulQA and MMLU benchmarks, measuring calibration with Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Brier score, and selective prediction metrics. Baselines are standard prompting (with and without confidence request) and state-of-the-art self-consistency sampling. Our main experiment measures if uncertainty-aware prompting yields better calibration and abstention behavior than baselines, especially on ambiguous questions.

per_idea.text: Uncertainty-Aware Prompting: Calibrating LLM Confidence via Explicit Abstention Queries Large language models often output predictions with overconfident probabilities, lacking reliable uncertainty estimates. This impedes safe deployment in settings where knowing when the model is unsure is critical. Recent advances in LLMs have achieved impressive accuracy, but their self-reported confidences are poorly calibrated, leading to misinformed decisions. Improving uncertainty quantification is timely as LLMs are increasingly used in high-stakes domains like medicine, law, and code generation. We propose an 'uncertainty-aware prompting' strategy that extends the prompt with explicit instructions allowing the model to abstain or express uncertainty. For each query, the prompt includes: (1) a request for both an answer and a confidence score, (2) an explicit option to reply 'I don't know' or 'uncertain' when appropriate, and (3) a brief context describing when to abstain. This prompt structure is designed to elicit more calibrated uncertainty estimates from the LLM without model finetuning. The predicted confidence is then mapped to a probability and tested for calibration. We evaluate on the TruthfulQA and MMLU benchmarks, measuring calibration with Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Brier score, and selective prediction metrics. Baselines are standard prompting (with and without confidence request) and state-of-the-art self-consistency sampling. Our main experiment measures if uncertainty-aware prompting yields better calibration and abstention behavior than baselines, especially on ambiguous questions.

idea.title: Self-Consistency Prompting for Uncertainty Calibration in Language Models
idea.problem: Large language models often exhibit overconfidence in their outputs, lacking calibrated uncertainty estimates. Existing prompting strategies do not adequately quantify or communicate model uncertainty.
idea.motivation: Downstream applications—such as medical QA or legal reasoning—require LLMs to express calibrated confidence for responsible deployment. Recent advances in prompting have improved accuracy, but uncertainty quantification remains underexplored and is increasingly critical as LLMs are deployed in high-stakes domains.
idea.method: We propose Self-Consistency Prompting (SCP), a simple method that elicits multiple diverse model responses to the same prompt using controlled sampling (e.g., temperature variation or instruction to 'answer independently'). The degree of agreement among responses is mapped to an uncertainty score. Optionally, the prompt can explicitly request a self-assessed confidence rating and justification, which is compared to the empirical agreement. This approach does not require model fine-tuning or access to logits, making it broadly applicable.
idea.experiment_plan: Test SCP on knowledge-intensive QA datasets (e.g., TriviaQA, SQuAD) using GPT-4. Baselines include direct confidence elicitation and logit-based uncertainty proxies. Evaluate calibration with Brier score and Expected Calibration Error (ECE), and assess whether self-consistency-based uncertainty correlates better with correctness than existing methods.

idea.title: Self-Consistency Prompting for Improved LLM Confidence Calibration
idea.problem: Large language models often produce overconfident predictions and unreliable uncertainty estimates in their outputs, hampering trustworthy deployment in sensitive domains. Efforts to calibrate model confidence within a single forward pass remain limited.
idea.motivation: Accurate uncertainty quantification is critical as LLMs are increasingly adopted for tasks such as medical QA, legal reasoning, and autonomous decision making. Existing approaches like temperature scaling or MC dropout are either coarse or computationally expensive, making efficient, prompt-based solutions timely and valuable.
idea.method: We propose self-consistency prompting, where the model is queried multiple times on the same input with diverse prompt templates (e.g., paraphrased questions or shuffled contexts). The agreement among sampled outputs serves as a direct proxy for the model's epistemic uncertainty, which is then explicitly reported alongside each prediction. This approach is designed to work with single-model APIs and does not require weight modification or extra training.
idea.experiment_plan: Test on open-domain QA datasets (SQuAD, TriviaQA) and a factuality estimation benchmark. Compare against single-pass confidence (softmax/maxprob) and other prompt-based calibration methods. Evaluate calibration with reliability diagrams, Expected Calibration Error (ECE), and coverage metrics for uncertainty estimates. A small experiment will use 20 questions with 5 prompt-variants each to assess if self-consistency correlates with actual answer correctness.

idea.title: Counterfactual Self-Consistency Prompts for LLM Confidence Calibration
idea.problem: LLMs remain poorly calibrated in their confidence, often overestimating or underestimating uncertainty in their outputs. Existing prompting methods for eliciting uncertainty are brittle and fail to generalize across domains.
idea.motivation: Reliable uncertainty estimates are critical for applications with high stakes, such as medical or legal domains. As LLMs integrate into decision pipelines, trustworthy confidence calibration is needed to avoid overreliance on incorrect predictions.
idea.method: We propose 'counterfactual self-consistency prompting': for each input, prompt the LLM to generate multiple answers after minor, controlled perturbations (e.g., synonyms, paraphrases) of the input. The LLM is then asked to provide a confidence estimate based on the observed agreement among its self-generated answers. This method leverages the model's internal consistency under distributional shifts as a proxy for epistemic uncertainty, reducing reliance on ungrounded likelihood scores.
idea.experiment_plan: Test on NLI (SNLI), QA (SQuAD), and arithmetic reasoning (GSM8K) with standardized calibration metrics (ECE, Brier Score). Baselines: vanilla confidence from logits, majority-vote self-consistency, and chain-of-thought self-consistency. Evaluate whether counterfactual self-consistency prompts improve calibration and uncertainty quantification versus baselines.

idea.title: Meta-Prompting: Improving LLM Uncertainty Calibration via Self-Evaluation Prompts
idea.problem: Large language models often exhibit poorly calibrated confidence in their outputs, especially on out-of-distribution or ambiguous queries. Current prompting methods provide only crude proxies for true uncertainty.
idea.motivation: Many downstream applications—such as medical QA or legal summarization—require trustworthy confidence estimates. LLMs' tendency to be overconfident undermines their safe use in such domains; thus, developing prompt-based uncertainty quantification tools is urgent and timely.
idea.method: We propose meta-prompting, a two-stage prompting scheme: (1) Generate an initial answer to a user query using the LLM, (2) Prompt the same LLM to self-evaluate the correctness and confidence of its answer, specifically requiring a justification for its self-assessed probability (e.g., 'What is the probability your answer is correct? Explain your reasoning.'). The meta-prompt leverages the model's internal knowledge and the context of the specific instance. This self-evaluation output is then calibrated (e.g., via temperature scaling or isotonic regression) using a held-out validation set.
idea.experiment_plan: We will evaluate on factual QA datasets (e.g., TriviaQA, Natural Questions) and OOD splits. Baselines include vanilla LLM confidence (e.g., log-prob of top token), and ensemble/MC dropout approaches. Metrics include Brier score, Expected Calibration Error (ECE), and coverage of confidence intervals. The decisive test is whether meta-prompted confidence estimates yield lower ECE than baselines.

idea.title: Prompting with Synthetic Counterfactuals for Improved LLM Uncertainty Calibration
idea.problem: Large language models (LLMs) often produce overconfident outputs, lacking meaningful uncertainty quantification. Existing prompting methods inadequately calibrate model confidence, especially on ambiguous or out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs.
idea.motivation: Reliable confidence estimates are critical for deploying LLMs in high-stakes domains. As LLMs are increasingly used for decision support and information retrieval, there is a growing need for methods that robustly calibrate their uncertainty, especially given their propensity to hallucinate or provide confident but incorrect answers.
idea.method: We propose Counterfactual Uncertainty Prompting (CUP): for each input, the prompt includes a synthetic counterfactual variation generated by minor perturbations (e.g., paraphrasing, named entity swaps). The model is asked to answer both the original and counterfactual, then reflect on any answer differences to explicitly rate its confidence in the original answer. The hypothesis is that the model's self-assessed confidence, when reflecting on its performance across perturbed variants, will better align with true uncertainty.
idea.experiment_plan: Test CUP on factual QA datasets (e.g., SQuAD, TriviaQA) and OOD QA subsets. Baselines include vanilla direct prompting and standard self-consistency methods. Metrics are confidence calibration error (e.g., Expected Calibration Error), accuracy, and OOD detection rate. Analyze calibration improvements, especially on ambiguous and counterfactual-altered queries.

idea.title: Self-Consistency Prompting for Uncertainty Quantification in Large Language Models
idea.problem: Current prompting methods provide unreliable measures of confidence or uncertainty in LLM outputs, often correlating poorly with actual correctness. This limits their safe deployment in high-stakes applications where calibrated uncertainty is essential.
idea.motivation: Effective uncertainty quantification is critical for applications like medical QA, legal advice, and autonomous agents. With the rise of LLMs in production systems, there is urgent need for prompting techniques that better reflect true model uncertainty and improve calibration, especially in few-shot or zero-shot regimes.
idea.method: We propose 'Self-Consistency Prompting', where the model is prompted multiple times with semantically equivalent but syntactically varied queries generated via paraphrasing or controlled perturbations of the prompt. The distribution of model responses across these prompts is used to empirically estimate uncertainty (e.g., by entropy or variance) and calibrate confidence. Additionally, we introduce a meta-prompting step that asks the model to reflect on the consistency of its own responses and revise its confidence accordingly.
idea.experiment_plan: We will evaluate the method on QA benchmarks (e.g., SQuAD, TruthfulQA, MedQA) and open-ended reasoning datasets. Baselines will include standard prompting, chain-of-thought, and existing uncertainty-aware approaches. Metrics will focus on calibration error (ECE), Brier score, and correlation between estimated uncertainty and ground-truth correctness.

idea.title: Self-Reflective Prompting: Enhancing Uncertainty Quantification in Language Models via Multi-Pass Metacognitive Queries
idea.problem: Current large language models (LLMs) often provide overconfident or poorly calibrated responses, lacking reliable mechanisms to express uncertainty in their outputs. There is a need for prompting strategies that elicit more accurate model introspection regarding its prediction confidence.
idea.motivation: Accurate uncertainty quantification is critical in high-stakes applications such as medicine, law, and scientific research, where overconfident errors can have severe consequences. With the rising adoption of LLMs in decision-making pipelines, systematically improving confidence calibration is both urgent and underexplored. Prompting methods present a flexible, model-agnostic route for interfacing with existing black-box LLMs.
idea.method: We propose 'Self-Reflective Prompting,' wherein each model response is followed by a meta-prompt that asks the LLM to explicitly evaluate its own certainty, enumerate plausible alternatives, and reason about potential sources of error. For example, after generating an answer, the model is prompted to provide a ranked list of alternative answers and assign likelihood estimates to each, along with justifications. This multi-pass, chained prompting approach leverages the model's latent uncertainty representations and encourages metacognitive reasoning, thereby producing calibrated confidence estimates without requiring model retraining.
idea.experiment_plan: We will evaluate our method on open-domain QA (e.g., TriviaQA, SQuAD), fact verification (FEVER), and commonsense reasoning datasets (e.g., CommonsenseQA). Baselines will include standard prompting with and without logit-based uncertainty, MC dropout ensembles, and self-consistency sampling. Metrics include Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Brier score, and selective prediction accuracy. Human-in-the-loop studies will assess whether the uncertainty outputs improve decision-making.

Baselines will include standard prompting with and without logit-based uncertainty, MC dropout ensembles, and self-consistency sampling. Metrics include Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Brier score, and selective prediction accuracy. Human-in-the-loop studies will assess whether the uncertainty outputs improve decision-making."
}

idea.title: Prompting LLMs with Self-Contradiction Detection for Improved Uncertainty Calibration
idea.problem: Current prompting methods for large language models (LLMs) often yield overconfident and poorly calibrated predictions, especially in open-domain or ambiguous settings. This impairs reliability in high-stakes applications where knowing model uncertainty is critical.
idea.motivation: Accurate quantification of uncertainty is essential for trustworthy LLM deployment in domains such as healthcare, law, and scientific research. Existing approaches like softmax probabilities or prompt ensembling are limited or inefficient. Prompt-based techniques could directly elicit uncertainty-aware outputs, taking advantage of recent LLM reasoning advancements.
idea.method: We propose a prompting framework that instructs LLMs to first answer a question, then generate a counter-argument or contradictory answer, and finally reflect on the divergence between both. The model is further prompted to rate its confidence based on its perceived internal consistency. This self-contradiction detection prompt is designed to expose model uncertainty by leveraging its ability to consider multiple perspectives and to self-assess answer quality. We aggregate the model's self-rated confidence with answer agreement or disagreement to produce a calibrated uncertainty estimate.
idea.experiment_plan: We evaluate on question-answering and commonsense reasoning datasets (e.g., TruthfulQA, OpenBookQA, and ARC). Baselines include standard direct-answer prompting, log probability-based confidence, and prompt ensembling. Calibration metrics (ECE, Brier score), answer accuracy, and correlation with human uncertainty judgments are measured to assess improvement in uncertainty quantification.

idea.title: Self-Prompted Ensembles: Improving Uncertainty Quantification in LLMs via Diverse Prompt Generation
idea.problem: Current LLM prompting methods yield overconfident outputs and poorly calibrated probabilities, limiting their reliability in critical applications. There is a need for prompting strategies that enable better uncertainty estimation without retraining the model.
idea.motivation: Reliable uncertainty quantification is essential for deploying LLMs in high-stakes domains like healthcare and law, where overconfidence can lead to harmful outcomes. Prompt-based ensembles are lightweight alternatives to model-based ensembling but lack systematic methods for maximizing diversity and informativeness. As LLMs grow in capability, improving calibration through prompt engineering becomes both timely and impactful.
idea.method: We propose a self-prompted ensemble method where the LLM first generates a set of diverse, high-quality prompts for a given task using explicit 'generate variants' instructions. Each generated prompt is then used to query the LLM independently, and the resulting outputs are aggregated to produce a consensus answer along with a calibrated uncertainty estimate, computed via entropy or variance across outputs. Crucially, the prompt-generation phase incorporates constraints (e.g., semantic diversity, syntactic variation) to maximize response diversity and reduce correlated errors. Optionally, a prompt selection stage filters out low-quality or redundant prompts before ensemble aggregation.
idea.experiment_plan: We evaluate on question answering (e.g., SQuAD, TriviaQA) and open-ended reasoning tasks, comparing against standard single-prompt and manually-crafted prompt ensemble baselines. Metrics include Brier score, expected calibration error (ECE), and coverage-accuracy tradeoffs. Human evaluation assesses interpretability of confidence estimates.

idea.title: Meta-Prompting: Uncertainty Calibration via In-Context Meta-Queries in Large Language Models
idea.problem: Large language models (LLMs) often produce overconfident outputs, lacking reliable intrinsic uncertainty estimates. Existing prompting techniques do not adequately calibrate model confidence or quantify prediction uncertainty.
idea.motivation: Calibrated uncertainty is critical for deploying LLMs in high-stakes domains such as medical QA, law, and autonomous agents. As LLMs are increasingly used as interactive tools, understanding and controlling their confidence is essential for responsible AI deployment and human trust.
idea.method: We propose a meta-prompting technique that augments standard task prompts with in-context meta-queries explicitly asking the LLM to self-assess its confidence. Each input is followed by a generated 'confidence probe' prompt that requests a probability estimate (e.g., 'On a scale of 0-100%, how confident are you in the above answer?'). Additionally, we calibrate these self-assessments by mixing few-shot examples with known ground-truth uncertainties and learning a mapping from raw confidence scores to calibrated probabilities. This approach enables fine-grained uncertainty estimation without model retraining or temperature tuning.
idea.experiment_plan: We will evaluate on open-domain QA (e.g., TriviaQA, NaturalQuestions) and reasoning datasets (e.g., GSM8K). Baselines include standard prompting, chain-of-thought with self-evaluation, and MC dropout/ensemble approaches (if available). Metrics include Brier score, ECE (Expected Calibration Error), coverage/accuracy curves, and qualitative case studies. Additional ablation studies will assess the impact of meta-query design and in-context calibration.
